topic: Deep learning on tabular data: learned tabular embeddings
scope: Applying DL to tabular data: FT-Transformer, TabNet, their role as feature extractors, comparison to gradient boosting, and link to entity embeddings.
key_concepts: Gradient boosting has long dominated tabular data, but deep learning is emerging as a powerful alternative by learning dense tabular embeddings.
Models like TabNet and FT-Transformer treat individual features (both numeric and categorical) within a row as tokens, similar to how NLP models process words.
These deep tabular networks can function as feature extractors, converting raw structured rows into rich, dense vector representations.
Gradient boosting typically still outperforms deep learning on small-to-medium datasets because it handles unscaled data, non-linear boundaries, and outliers naturally.
Deep tabular models become advantageous when the dataset is exceptionally large, when tabular data needs to be fused with text or images, or when building a continuous representation learning system.

definitions.definition: A neural network architecture that adapts the transformer attention mechanism to process the columns of a tabular dataset.

examples: Passing a row of patient health metrics through FT-Transformer to extract a single dense patient embedding, which is then used by a simpler downstream prediction model.

relations.precedes: ch15_s08 (combining these learned features with engineered ones).
relations.follows: Chapter 4 (which introduced entity embeddings specifically for categorical variables).

exclusions: Do not explain the complex internal gating mechanisms of TabNet or the self-attention matrices of FT-Transformer.
